claim: Strategic programming (long-term architectural thinking) is more important than tactical programming (day-to-day coding) in the AI era, since AI excels at the tactical work
actionable: Focus your effort on designing codebase architecture, scoping tasks, and defining interfaces between modules—delegate implementation details to AI
source_title: Matt Pocock's Agentic Engineering Workflow (just copy him)

claim: Good software fundamentals (codebase design, testing, documentation) directly improve AI's ability to work (AX/agent experience) and reduce token spend
actionable: Invest in clean codebase architecture, clear interfaces, good test coverage, and localized documentation—this lets cheaper models succeed with fewer tokens
source_title: Matt Pocock's Agentic Engineering Workflow (just copy him)

claim: Skills should be procedures (user-invoked, not automatically triggered) rather than abilities (auto-invoked by the model) to maintain control over your thinking
actionable: Structure your agent setup with user-controlled procedure skills you invoke explicitly (like grill-me for interviews) rather than abilities that auto-trigger
source_title: Matt Pocock's Agentic Engineering Workflow (just copy him)

claim: AFK (away-from-keyboard) agents parallelized on GitHub Actions or sandboxes outperform synchronous human-in-the-loop workflows for scoped tasks
actionable: Set up AFK agents in GitHub Actions (with labels like 'agent implement') or sandboxes like Sand Castle to parallelize work and reduce blocking on permissions/interactions
source_title: Matt Pocock's Agentic Engineering Workflow (just copy him)

claim: Queues (GitHub issues, backlogs) are more practical than continuous loops (while-true prompting) for organizing agent work across teams
actionable: Organize agent work as a queue (GitHub issues with agent labels) rather than as infinite loops; pull tasks off the queue, agents resolve them asynchronously
source_title: Matt Pocock's Agentic Engineering Workflow (just copy him)

claim: The teach skill uses zone of proximal development, knowledge/skills/wisdom distinction, and stateful learning records to personalize education by mission-first design
actionable: When teaching yourself with an AI, start by clarifying your mission (what are you building?) and let the AI orient you in the problem space before jumping to content
source_title: Matt Pocock's Agentic Engineering Workflow (just copy him)

claim: Waiting ~1 month after a new model release (e.g., Fable, Opus 4.5) to assess adoption risk is safer than immediately switching for production work
actionable: When a new model drops, don't immediately replace your production setup; wait a month to see how the ecosystem reacts, then decide on migration
source_title: Matt Pocock's Agentic Engineering Workflow (just copy him)

claim: Dictation (speech-to-text like Whisper Flow) is a high-leverage skill for developers because it dramatically speeds up expressing ideas and training models
actionable: Adopt dictation tooling (Whisper Flow, etc.) and practice verbalizing your thoughts fluidly; this becomes a compounding skill for faster thought-to-model iteration
source_title: Matt Pocock's Agentic Engineering Workflow (just copy him)

claim: Senior developers see 10x productivity gains with AI; juniors see smaller gains. The multiplier effect depends on domain expertise and understanding of your codebase architecture
actionable: If hiring for AI-native roles, prioritize domain expertise and architectural thinking over pure AI tooling familiarity; train the latter
source_title: Matt Pocock's Agentic Engineering Workflow (just copy him)

claim: Harness optimization (prompts, skills, codebase design, environment) matters equally to model quality; focus on what you can control rather than chasing new models
actionable: Split effort 50/50 between model selection and harness optimization; improve your codebase, skills, and prompts as much as you wait for better models
source_title: Matt Pocock's Agentic Engineering Workflow (just copy him)

claim: Start with a blank-slate agent config (no skills, no MCPs, empty CLAUDE.md), observe default behavior, then layer on only the tools you actually miss
actionable: Delete your entire agent config, start from scratch, and deliberately add back only the skills/tools you genuinely need—avoid context bloat
source_title: Matt Pocock's Agentic Engineering Workflow (just copy him)

claim: You should know your product better than the AI; delegate tactical execution but retain strategic vision for what features to build and why
actionable: Before asking an AI to build a feature, articulate your vision, the 10 most consequential design decisions, and your mission—interview yourself with the AI to align
source_title: Matt Pocock's Agentic Engineering Workflow (just copy him)

claim: Build human-in-the-loop checkpoints at key steps (security review, QA, merge approval) but aim to remove them over time using pattern-based AI review systems
actionable: Start with heavy review, then train the AI to classify low-risk PRs (refactors, internal fixes); monitor the classifier itself by sampling its 'low-risk' approvals
source_title: Matt Pocock's Agentic Engineering Workflow (just copy him)

claim: Product design, customer research, and vision are things AI cannot do for you; go talk to real customers to understand what to build
actionable: Use AI for implementation speed, but invest your own time in talking to customers, understanding their pain, and designing solutions—AI augments but doesn't replace product thinking
source_title: Matt Pocock's Agentic Engineering Workflow (just copy him)
